summary: Gruber reviews The Daily iPad app three days after launch and finds it fatally flawed by its loading time. The app takes one minute and twenty seconds to display any content, which he argues is an unacceptable user experience regardless of how good the content might be. He compares it unfavorably to the NYT iPad app (25 seconds) and Flipboard (7 seconds), establishing a clear performance hierarchy. He rejects the justification that long reading sessions make a one-minute load acceptable, arguing the first minute is the most important. He proposes practical solutions like progressive loading and editorial prioritization of above-the-fold content. His core argument is that native apps must provide a better experience than websites, and an 80-second loading spinner fails that test entirely.
key_points: The Daily takes 80 seconds to display any content, compared to 25 seconds for the NYT app and 7 seconds for Flipboard
No amount of good content or design can compensate for an unacceptable wait before the reader sees anything at all
The app should use progressive loading — showing the most important story first while downloading the rest in the background
The justification that long reading sessions offset slow load times is wrong because first impressions determine whether readers return
Native apps that perform worse than loading a website in Mobile Safari have failed at their fundamental purpose
money_quotes: Imagine a paper newspaper that was wrapped in an envelope, and the envelope was so difficult to open that it took over a minute before you could see the front page of the issue. Who would buy that newspaper? No one, that's who.
The first minute is the most important minute. That's the minute where the reader makes their impression of The Daily.
The point of native apps is to provide a better experience than websites can offer. Watching a splash screen rotate a "Loading…" spinner for 80 seconds isn't it.
At that point, it's already a lost cause. There's nothing the actual content or interface of the app can do to make up for the fact that it takes way too long to see anything at all.
Why wait for The Daily when you can open Mobile Safari and see the home page for any of your favorite news sites in 10 seconds?
key_insight: An app's loading time isn't a minor technical detail but the single most important moment in the user experience, because readers who wait too long simply won't come back.
